skill.name: c4-modeling
skill.description: Create C4 architecture diagrams and documentation using level-specific guidance, review checkpoints, and Mermaid C4 syntax examples.
skill.summary: Guides C4 architecture modeling from system context through component and code views.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but manual review found they are false positives from Markdown code fences, C4 syntax examples, and documentation text. No executable scripts, command execution, cryptography, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or network behavior were found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The scanner treated Markdown backtick fences and Mermaid examples as Ruby or shell execution. These lines are documentation examples for C4 diagrams and do not define executable commands or scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is fenced Markdown and Mermaid C4 syntax. There is no shell interpreter, Ruby runtime, script file, or instruction to execute commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Diagram Terms Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings appear to match ordinary documentation words such as Description, design, and diagram syntax. No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage appears in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found architecture documentation and Mermaid element descriptions only. I did not find evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, cipher configuration, or password handling.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Links Flagged as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The network findings are a sample SMTP relationship label and three external resource links. They do not perform network requests, send email, collect data, or contact external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain Markdown and Mermaid labels. There is no executable network client, fetch call, credential use, or email-sending implementation.

content.value_statement: Architecture discussions often fail when diagrams mix too many levels of detail. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create clear C4 views with Mermaid syntax and review checkpoints.
actual_capabilities: Creates C4 context, container, component, and selective code-level diagram plans.
Uses Mermaid C4 syntax examples for people, systems, containers, components, and relationships.
Applies checkpoints to avoid mixing abstraction levels in one diagram.
Adds element descriptions, relationship labels, and technology choices where appropriate.
Provides level-specific review guidance for architecture documentation.
limitations: Does not inspect a codebase automatically unless the user provides project context.
Does not validate Mermaid rendering in a live diagram tool by itself.
Does not replace architecture review by domain owners or security teams.
Code-level diagrams are intended only for critical or complex areas.

use_cases.title: Map a New Product Architecture
use_cases.description: Create context and container views before implementation so teams align on boundaries, actors, systems, and integrations.

use_cases.title: Document an Existing Service
use_cases.description: Turn service knowledge into C4 diagrams that show major containers, dependencies, data stores, and labeled relationships.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Architecture Review
use_cases.description: Build diagrams at the right abstraction level for stakeholder review, technical discussion, or onboarding material.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Context Diagram
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a high-level system view.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Level 1 C4 context diagram for this system: [system name]. Users are [actors]. External systems are [dependencies]. Include short descriptions and labeled relationships.

prompt_templates.title: Expand to Containers
prompt_templates.scenario: Move from system context to major runtime parts.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Level 2 C4 container diagram for [system name]. Include applications, APIs, databases, queues, technology choices, and relationships. Do not include internal modules.

prompt_templates.title: Review Diagram Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Check an existing architecture diagram for C4 clarity.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this C4 diagram for level consistency, missing descriptions, unclear relationships, too many elements, and missing technology choices: [diagram or description].

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Level Architecture Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a C4 documentation set for [system]. Start with context, then identify which containers need component diagrams. Recommend code-level diagrams only where complexity justifies them.

output_examples.input: A product team needs a C4 context diagram for an e-commerce platform.
output: Identifies customers, administrators, the e-commerce platform, payment, shipping, and email systems.
Defines labeled relationships such as browsing, order management, payment processing, shipment creation, and transactional messaging.
Keeps databases and implementation details out of the context view.

output_examples.input: An API team wants a container diagram for a web and mobile commerce system.
output: Separates web application, mobile app, API server, worker, database, cache, and message queue containers.
Adds technology choices for each major runtime element.
Shows external payment integration without exposing component internals.

output_examples.input: A lead asks whether a component or code-level diagram is appropriate.
output: Recommends a component diagram for modules, services, repositories, and external integrations.
Recommends a code-level diagram only for critical algorithms, key interfaces, or complex data structures.
Flags simple utilities and standard CRUD flows as poor candidates for code-level diagrams.

best_practices: Start with a context diagram before creating deeper C4 levels.
Keep each diagram at one abstraction level with clear element descriptions.
Label every relationship and add technology choices for container and component views.
anti_patterns: Mixing containers, components, and classes in one diagram.
Adding databases or implementation details to a context diagram.
Creating code-level diagrams for simple or frequently changing code.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps create C4 architecture diagrams and related documentation using level-specific guidance and Mermaid syntax.

faq.question: Which C4 levels does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers context, container, component, and selective code-level views.

faq.question: Does it generate Mermaid diagrams?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes Mermaid C4 syntax patterns for elements, boundaries, relationships, and examples.

faq.question: Can it use Structurizr or PlantUML?
faq.answer: The skill lists those formats as options, but the included syntax reference focuses on Mermaid C4.

faq.question: Does it analyze source code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It needs project context from the user or repository exploration by the assistant.

faq.question: When should code-level diagrams be used?
faq.answer: Use them only for critical, complex, or architecturally important code that benefits from explicit documentation.
